section.title: The Jew and the Law
section.sectionRationale: Paul now names the moralist he has been addressing: the Jew who leans on the law, boasts in God, and is confident he is a guide to the blind. Paul grants every privilege, then presses a single devastating question: do you keep the law you teach? The one who boasts in the law dishonors God by breaking it, so that God's name is blasphemed among the nations. Circumcision in the flesh means nothing without obedience; true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code.
section.thesis: Covenant privilege, the possession of the law, and the outward mark of circumcision cannot save a person who breaks the law; God looks for a reality of the heart, an inward and Spirit-wrought devotion, and He gives His praise not to the outwardly religious but to the inwardly transformed.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Jew who relies on the law and boasts in God; the searching questions that expose the teacher who does not practice what he preaches; and the redefinition of the true Jew and true circumcision as a matter of the heart, by the Spirit.

units.label: The Jew who relies on the law

verses.text: Now you, if you call yourself a {{Ioudaios|Jew}}; if you rely on the {{nomos|law}} and boast in God;
if you know His will and approve of what is superior because you are instructed by the law;
if you are convinced that you are a guide for the blind, a light for those in darkness,
an instructor of the foolish, a teacher of infants, because you have in the law the embodiment of knowledge and truth:

units.label: You who teach others, do you not teach yourself?
units.note: the boast in the law contradicted by breaking it

verses.text: you, then, who teach others, do you not teach yourself? You who preach against stealing, do you steal?
You who forbid adultery, do you commit adultery? You who abhor idols, do you rob temples?
You who boast in the law, do you dishonor God by breaking the law?
As it is written: “God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you.”

units.label: True circumcision is of the heart
units.note: inward and by the Spirit, not by the written code

verses.text: {{peritome|Circumcision}} has value if you observe the law, but if you break the law, your circumcision has become uncircumcision.
If a man who is not circumcised keeps the requirements of the law, will not his uncircumcision be regarded as circumcision?
The one who is physically uncircumcised yet keeps the law will condemn you who, even though you have the written code and circumcision, are a lawbreaker.
A man is not a Jew because he is one outwardly, nor is circumcision only outward and physical.

No, a man is a Jew because he is one inwardly, and circumcision is a matter of the heart, by the {{pneuma|Spirit}}, not by the {{gramma|written code}}. Such a man's praise does not come from men, but from God.

Ioudaios.gloss: A Jew, a member of the covenant people descended from Judah. Paul finally names the moralist he has been addressing. To be called a Jew was to claim a real and God-given privilege, but Paul will argue that the name carries no weight before God unless it is matched by an inward reality, redefining the true Jew as one inwardly, not merely by descent.

nomos.gloss: Law, especially the law of Moses, the Torah. The Jew relies on the law and boasts in possessing it. Paul does not deny its glory, but he insists that resting in the law without keeping it is worthless; the law he trusts becomes the very measure that condemns him as a lawbreaker.

peritome.gloss: Circumcision, the covenant sign given to Abraham, cut in the flesh of every Israelite male. Paul grants its value if the law is kept, but warns that a lawbreaker's circumcision counts as uncircumcision. The outward sign without the inward reality is empty; God seeks circumcision of the heart.

pneuma.gloss: Spirit, here the Holy Spirit as the agent of true, inward circumcision. Real covenant membership is a work of the Spirit on the heart, not merely a mark on the body. This points forward to the new covenant, in which God writes His law within and gives His Spirit to His people.

gramma.gloss: Letter or written code, the law as external written instruction. Paul contrasts the mere letter with the Spirit: circumcision by the written code is outward and physical, but circumcision by the Spirit is inward and real. The letter can command but cannot transform; only the Spirit gives the changed heart God requires.

basic.context: Paul has been dismantling the confidence of the moralist who judges others while doing the same things. Now he addresses that person by name: the Jew who trusts in the law and the covenant. This was the person most likely to feel exempt from chapter 1's indictment, since Israel possessed the very words of God. Paul honors that privilege and then shows that privilege without obedience only deepens guilt.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage is built on a series of if clauses, verses 17 to 20, that list the Jew's real advantages, followed by a series of stinging questions, verses 21 to 23, that expose the gap between teaching and doing. Then Paul redefines Jew and circumcision, moving from the outward and physical to the inward and Spirit-wrought. Read the argument as a whole: privilege is real, but it does not exempt; it intensifies responsibility.
basic.originalLanguages: To be a Ioudaios, a Jew, and to hold the nomos, the law, were genuine privileges. But peritome, circumcision in the flesh, without obedience becomes uncircumcision. The true circumcision is by the pneuma, the Spirit, not by the gramma, the mere written code. God's praise falls on the inward reality, not the outward badge.
basic.typology: Circumcision of the flesh, the sign given to Abraham, points beyond itself to circumcision of the heart, promised in Deuteronomy and fulfilled by the Spirit in the new covenant. The physical sign was always meant to signify an inner cutting away of hardness and sin, and Paul insists the sign is empty without the substance.
basic.christ: Paul does not name Christ directly here, but the whole passage prepares for Him. The heart circumcised by the Spirit is the work of the new covenant that Christ inaugurates by His blood and pours out at Pentecost. The praise that comes from God, not from men, is granted only to those united to Christ, whose Spirit writes God's law within.
basic.god: God is not impressed by outward badges or inherited privileges. He looks on the heart, values obedience over profession, and reserves His praise for the inward and real. He is jealous for His own name, which is blasphemed among the nations when His people boast in the law yet break it. He seeks worshipers in Spirit and in truth.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 30:6 promises that the LORD will circumcise your heart. Jeremiah 9:25-26 warns of those circumcised only in the flesh. Isaiah 52:5 laments that God's name is blasphemed among the nations. Philippians 3:3 declares that we are the circumcision who worship by the Spirit and put no confidence in the flesh.
basic.oneStory: God gave Israel the law and the covenant sign as precious gifts pointing to a deeper reality. The prophets had always warned that an uncircumcised heart made the outward sign empty. Paul now brings that warning to its climax: covenant privilege cannot save the disobedient, and the true people of God are those whose hearts the Spirit has circumcised, a people gathered in Christ from Jew and Gentile alike.

context.literary: Romans 2:17-29 completes the indictment of the moralist begun at 2:1 by finally naming him as the Jew who relies on the law. The unit moves from a catalogue of privileges to a barrage of accusing questions to a redefinition of what it means to be a true Jew, ending on the note of praise that comes from God rather than men.
context.historical: Paul writes to a Roman church of Jewish and Gentile believers around AD 57. Jewish identity centered on the law and circumcision, marks of covenant belonging and sources of pride. Paul, himself a Jew, engages this confidence from within, insisting that God's ancient prophets had already relativized the outward sign in favor of the heart.
context.canonical: The passage stands in continuity with the prophets who called for circumcised hearts and with the New Testament teaching that outward religion without inward reality is worthless. It sets up chapter 3, where Paul will still affirm the real advantage of the Jew while insisting that Jew and Gentile alike are under sin and justified only by faith.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Paul intends to show the law-trusting Jew that possessing and teaching the law, and bearing the sign of circumcision, cannot substitute for actually keeping the law and having a heart devoted to God, so that this reader too is brought under judgment and pointed toward a righteousness not his own.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the failure of Israel's privileged to live up to their calling. It prescribes for all religious people in every age a warning against resting in outward marks, roles, and knowledge while the heart remains unchanged, and a call to seek the inward reality that only the Spirit gives.

debates.question: Who is the uncircumcised man who keeps the law and condemns the lawbreaking Jew in verses 26 and 27?

views.label: The Spirit-transformed Gentile believer
views.case: Many read this as the Gentile Christian, uncircumcised in the flesh but circumcised in heart by the Spirit, whose obedient faith shames the disobedient Jew. This fits Paul's redefinition of the true Jew as one inwardly.

views.label: A hypothetical righteous Gentile
views.case: Others take it as a hypothetical case pressing the logic that God values obedience over the outward sign, without necessarily identifying a specific group; the point is the principle that the sign without obedience is empty.

debates.bearing: On either reading Paul overturns confidence in physical circumcision; what counts is a heart that keeps God's will, which prepares for the gospel truth that such hearts are created only by the Spirit in Christ.

debates.question: Is Paul denying any real advantage to being a Jew?

views.label: Privilege is real but insufficient
views.case: The historic reading holds that Paul affirms genuine covenant privilege, which he states plainly in 3:1-2, while denying that privilege without obedience can save. The advantage is real but cannot exempt from judgment.

views.label: Privilege is wholly relativized
views.case: Some emphasize how thoroughly Paul empties outward Jewishness of saving value, redefining the true Jew inwardly, so that ethnic and ritual identity is reframed almost entirely around the heart.

debates.bearing: Both agree that outward Jewishness cannot justify; the balance Paul strikes, real advantage yet no exemption, guards against both despising the covenant and trusting in it apart from faith.

study.originalLanguages: The passage pivots on names and signs. To be a Ioudaios, a Jew, and to possess the nomos, the law, were real privileges, but Paul shows they cannot exempt the disobedient. Peritome, circumcision in the flesh, becomes uncircumcision when the law is broken, for God seeks a deeper cutting away. The true circumcision is by the pneuma, the Spirit, not by the gramma, the mere written code that can command but cannot change the heart. The one so transformed receives praise not from men but from God.

typology.fulfillment: The covenant sign given to Abraham points to circumcision of the heart, promised in Deuteronomy 30:6 and worked by the Spirit in the new covenant.
Israel according to the flesh points to the true Israel, those inwardly transformed, gathered from Jew and Gentile in Christ, Philippians 3:3.
The written code that commands without transforming points to the Spirit of the new covenant who writes God's law on the heart, 2 Corinthians 3:6.

study.christ: Christ is not named in these verses, yet the whole passage leans toward Him. The circumcision of the heart by the Spirit is precisely the gift of the new covenant that Jesus secured by His death and poured out at Pentecost. The praise that comes from God rather than men is granted only to those hidden in Christ, whose righteousness is not their own. Paul is clearing away every false ground of confidence so that the reader, Jew or Gentile, will look for acceptance not in law or lineage or ritual but in the crucified and risen Lord who alone can make the heart new.
study.god: God searches beyond appearances to the heart. He is not moved by inherited status, religious knowledge, or ceremonial marks when the life contradicts them. He treasures obedience over profession and reserves His praise for the inward and genuine. He is also jealous for His own reputation among the nations, grieved when those who bear His law dishonor Him by breaking it. This is the God who seeks worshipers in Spirit and in truth and who gives His Spirit to create the very devotion He requires.

crossReferences.note: The LORD your God will circumcise your heart, the promise behind Paul's inward circumcision by the Spirit.
God will punish those circumcised only in the flesh, for the whole house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart.
My name is continually blasphemed, the text Paul cites against those whose disobedience dishonors God among the nations.
We are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and glory in Christ Jesus, putting no confidence in the flesh.

study.oneStory: God gave Israel the law and the sign of circumcision as gifts pointing forward to a heart made new. From Moses and the prophets onward, He warned that an uncircumcised heart rendered the outward sign hollow. Paul brings that long warning to its head: covenant privilege cannot save the disobedient. The story is moving toward a new covenant people, circumcised in heart by the Spirit, drawn from Jew and Gentile alike, who receive their praise from God and glory only in Christ.

quiz.q: What advantages does Paul grant to the person who calls himself a Jew?

options.text: He relies on the law, boasts in God, knows His will, and is instructed by the law.
options.note: Verses 17 and 18 list exactly these real, God-given privileges.

options.text: He possesses the law and genuine knowledge of God's revealed will.
options.note: True as far as it goes, though Paul's list is fuller, including boasting in God and guiding others.

options.text: He is automatically righteous before God because of his ancestry.
options.note: Paul grants privileges but denies that they make anyone righteous apart from obedience.

options.text: He has no advantage of any kind over the Gentiles.
options.note: Paul affirms real advantages here, and states them plainly again in 3:1-2.

quiz.q: What is the sharp contradiction Paul exposes in verses 21 to 23?

options.text: The one who teaches and boasts in the law dishonors God by breaking it.
options.note: Paul's questions expose the gap between teaching the law and keeping it.

options.text: The teacher of the law fails to practice what he preaches to others.
options.note: Accurate, though the fuller point is that this breaking of the law dishonors God.

options.text: The Jew teaches the law too strictly for others to follow.
options.note: Paul's charge is not strictness but hypocrisy, teaching what he does not do.

options.text: The Jew refuses to teach the law to the Gentiles at all.
options.note: The problem is not withholding teaching but failing to live it out.

quiz.q: According to Paul, what happens to circumcision if a person breaks the law?

options.text: His circumcision has become uncircumcision; the outward sign counts as nothing.
options.note: Verse 25 states this directly: lawbreaking empties the sign of value.

options.text: The sign loses its worth because obedience, not the mark, is what matters.
options.note: Correct in principle; the text puts it more starkly as circumcision becoming uncircumcision.

options.text: His circumcision still guarantees his standing regardless of his conduct.
options.note: The reverse of Paul's argument; the sign without obedience is worthless.

options.text: Circumcision was never of any value to anyone at any time.
options.note: Paul says it has value if you observe the law; the problem is lawbreaking.

quiz.q: How does Paul define the true Jew and true circumcision?

options.text: A Jew inwardly, whose circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit, not by the written code.
options.note: Verse 29 gives precisely this inward, Spirit-wrought definition.

options.text: One whose heart is changed within rather than merely marked outwardly.
options.note: Right in substance, though it omits the key phrase by the Spirit, not by the written code.

options.text: One who keeps the ceremonial law more carefully than others.
options.note: Paul locates true circumcision in the heart by the Spirit, not in ceremonial rigor.

options.text: Anyone born into the nation of Israel, without exception.
options.note: Paul explicitly denies that a man is a Jew merely because he is one outwardly.

quiz.q: Whose praise does the truly circumcised person receive?

options.text: Praise from God, not from men.
options.note: Verse 29 ends exactly here: such a man's praise comes from God, not men.

options.text: Approval from God rather than the applause of people.
options.note: Correct in meaning; the text states it as praise from God, not from men.

options.text: Recognition from the religious community for his obedience.
options.note: Paul contrasts God's praise with human recognition, not centers it there.

options.text: No praise at all, since God never commends anyone.
options.note: The verse says the opposite: his praise comes from God.

questions: Paul lists real privileges the Jew enjoyed, yet warns they could not save. What spiritual advantages or knowledge might you be quietly resting in instead of resting in Christ?
The teacher who preached against stealing was stealing. Where is there a gap between what you tell others is right and what you actually practice?
God's name can be dishonored among outsiders by the inconsistency of His people. How might your daily conduct be shaping the way others think about God?
Paul says true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit. What is the difference between outward religious activity and an inwardly changed heart, and which describes you?
The truly transformed person seeks praise from God rather than from people. Whose approval are you actually living for this week?

translationNotes.note: The BSB ends this verse with an em dash leading into verse 21. It is rendered here with a colon to preserve the flow of the sentence while avoiding the dash.
God's name is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you draws on Isaiah 52:5. Paul turns the lament into an indictment: Israel's disobedience causes the nations to speak against God.

By the Spirit, not by the written code translates a contrast between pneuma and gramma. The phrase can be read as by the Spirit rather than the letter, pointing to the inward, Spirit-wrought reality of the new covenant.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 2:17-29. The em dash at the end of verse 20 was rendered as a colon to avoid a dash while preserving the flow into verse 21. Footnote markers and the Isaiah 52:5 citation note were omitted from the running text per format. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
